steps.title: Identify the grammar rule
steps.content: The phrase "Even back in the 1860s" sets the action firmly in the past. The verb must agree with the past-time context and the singular subject (nearly 30% of the population, treated as a singular quantity).

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'is being': violates tense agreement — present progressive tense refers to an action happening now, contradicting "back in the 1860s." ✗
  • Choice B — 'was': correct — simple past tense matches the 1860s time reference and agrees with the singular subject. ✓
  • Choice C — 'is': violates tense agreement — simple present tense refers to a current state, contradicting "back in the 1860s." ✗
  • Choice D — 'will be': violates tense agreement — future tense refers to something yet to happen, contradicting the past time reference. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
